title: A World of Strangers
description: A South African novel about race, privilege, segregation, and social distance under apartheid.
descriptionParagraphs: A South African novel about race, privilege, segregation, and social distance under apartheid.

A World of Strangers is usually read through its treatment of apartheid, race, and liberal critique. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes apartheid, race, and liberal critique feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A South African novel about race, privilege, segregation, and social distance under apartheid.
banningParagraphs: A World of Strangers entered censorship debates as a novel associated with apartheid, race, and liberal critique. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around racial politics and anti state.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1958 onward in South Africa, where Apartheid censors banned circulation. The novel's interracial social world was unacceptable under apartheid censorship rules. Gordimer's career repeatedly collided with a state determined to police representation itself.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Everybody's Protest Novel
counterReadings.author: James Baldwin
counterReadings.note: A skeptical response to the politics of moral uplift in canonical anti-slavery fiction.

counterReadings.title: The Wretched of the Earth
counterReadings.author: Frantz Fanon

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
